channel.title: CGD Blog (Audio)
channel.description: Center for Global Development blog posts, converted to audio.
channel.itunes:author: Center for Global Development

item.title: What Does Philanthropy’s Global Footprint Tell Us About Its Role in a Changing Aid Landscape?
item.description: The recent decline in official development assistance has given new urgency to a familiar question: can private philanthropy help fill the gap? The likely answer is: not at the scale required. Private foundations cannot replace public aid budgets and should not be treated as a substitute for predict

item.title: Missing the Forest for the Trees: School Meals, MDBs, and the Limits of Siloed Development Finance
Too Cavalier About Conflicts of Interest at the World Bank?
Three Issues to Watch at WHA79
From Pipeline to Power: What We Learned at CGD's First Annual Women in Leadership Conference
item.description: The title of CGD's first annual Women in Leadership Conference, “ From Pipeline to Power ,” points to a seemingly universal pattern. Across international financial institutions, economics departments, corporate boards, and ministerial cabinets , the pipeline of women exists. Women enter these instit

item.title: Amendments to the Code Regulating Health Worker Mobility Were Adopted at the World Health Assembly. Now What?
item.description: At the 79th World Health Assembly, Member States adopted landmark amendments to the World Health Organization’s (WHO) Global Code of Practice on the International Recruitment of Health Personnel. But what were these changes, and how can they be operationalised in practice? Understanding the WHO Glob

item.title: The Developing World’s Jobs Crisis Was Here Before AI
item.description: Developing countries need to create roughly 800 million more jobs over the next decade than they are on track to produce. That gap is already there before a single algorithm has replaced a single worker. Of the 1.2 billion young people who will reach working age over that decade, only about 420 mill

item.title: The EU’s Global Europe Instrument: Ambition, Tension, and What Needs to Change
item.description: The European Union (EU) is redesigning how it finances development around the world. The proposed Global Europe Instrument will shape where EU money goes, who benefits, and what strings, if any, are attached. With the Foreign Affairs Council meeting on 18 May, this is the moment to ask whether the p

item.title: Donors Are Increasingly Focusing on “Systems Strengthening”: How Can They Do It Well?
item.description: Growth in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), combined with declines in aid spending, means the vast majority of development spending, except in the poorest countries, comes from governments and individuals in the countries themselves. This has been true for some time, but recent aid cuts unde

item.title: Can Nutritional Supplements Reduce Lead Poisoning?
item.description: Widespread, low-level lead exposure costs children millions of IQ points and massive cumulative increases in health risks. How can we get the lead out? The top priority is prevention: by avoiding and removing sources of exposure. The next is treatment: but chelation drugs are expensive, and reserved

item.title: Skills for Jobs in Sub-Saharan Africa: An Urgent, Pressing Agenda
item.description: More than one in five youth across Africa are neither in education nor working. Medium and large firms continue to point to an inadequately skilled workforce as a constraint to productivity. Only a small fraction of children are able to read and understand a simple sentence by age ten, a building bl

item.title: Oil at $100 a Barrel: Fiscal Strain and Risks of Social Unrest
item.description: Shielding households from the recent surge in oil prices could impose high fiscal costs on governments—about 0.9 percent of GDP in emerging and developing economies and 0.4 percent of GDP in advanced economies. Some highly vulnerable countries could face costs up to 3 percent of GDP. The war in the

item.title: Will the Iran War Be the Breaking Point for Vulnerable Countries?
item.description: Energy prices have spiked with the near-closure of the Strait of Hormuz and ongoing strikes on regional energy infrastructure. Even before this shock, many developing countries were increasingly vulnerable, pressured by high debt burdens, an unpredictable global trade environment, and unfavorable ex

item.title: Beyond Oil: The Budgetary Costs of Conflict in the Middle East
item.description: In a recent blog post , we argued that the increase in oil prices spurred by the war in the Middle East—particularly if sustained—will likely have major consequences for government budgets. Many governments, in both advanced and developing economies, are cushioning households and firms from higher e

item.title: The Iran War Oil Shock Will Hit the Hungry Hardest
item.description: The Iran war has already led to a spike in oil prices. While wealthier countries focus on fuel prices, for those in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), this could mean something far worse : a sudden increase in hunger and poverty, as rising food prices erode purchasing power. We are already se

item.title: Stress-Tested by War: Modernizing IMF Support in a Volatile World
item.description: Last week, the IMF published a blog post with its first assessment of the economic fallout from the Iran war. The blog post carries immense institutional weight—co-authored by all the Fund’s area department directors, the chief economist, and the heads of monetary/capital markets and fiscal affairs.

item.title: Between Iran and a Hard Place
item.description: Stakeholders gathering for the upcoming Spring Meetings of the IMF and World Bank will be contending with yet another major crisis—war in the Middle East—with ramifications expected to include dampened growth, inflation, and tighter financial conditions that will reverberate far beyond the zone of c

item.title: CGD Podcast: What Does the Iran War Mean for Low- and Middle-Income Countries?
item.description: Clemence Landers: Welcome to the CGD podcast. I'm Clemence Landers, vice president and senior fellow at the Center for Global Development. Today, we are talking about the economic ramifications of the war in Iran on emerging markets and low-income countries. The Strait of Hormuz, one of the most con

item.title: Doing the Right Thing at a Cost
item.description: The Middle East war has triggered a sharp contraction in the oil supply that passes through the Strait of Hormuz, driving up global energy prices and forcing governments across the world to choose how much of that cost to pass on to households. Their responses have diverged markedly. Because energy

item.title: What Are We Watching for at the UK’s Global Partnerships Conference?
item.description: From signals to substance on development cooperation Next week, the UK co-hosts its conference on the future of international development. Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper will host the event, alongside South Africa, the Children’s Investment Fund Foundation and the UK’s impact investor and developme

item.title: Reconciling Sovereign Debt Reform Proposals with Impact and Reality
item.description: For many developing countries, the ongoing conflict in the Middle East is yet another shock to already fragile public finances. Higher energy, food, and fertilizer prices are worsening balance-of-payments pressures, raising inflation risks, and forcing difficult tradeoffs between debt service and es

item.title: Millions of Children Go to School Near a Toxic Site
item.description: Millions of children in low- and middle-income countries attend school near sites known to be contaminated by toxic pollutants such as lead, mercury, arsenic, and pesticides. In a new paper , I show exactly where. We already knew that lead exposure hurts learning , but it wasn’t clear how schools co

item.title: When Is a Country Too Rich for Aid? Rethinking ODA Eligibility — contrast, the majority of bilateral aid from DAC members is in the form of grants, and ODA is explicitly about the welfare of people in developing countries. And in that context, a measure of income that takes into account purchasing power is much more relevant (it is notable that in calculating poverty estimates, the World Bank uses the PPP measure).
item.description: The OECD’s Development Assistance Committee (DAC) is discussing changes to the rules on which countries are eligible to receive official development assistance (ODA), the primary measure of foreign aid. Currently, countries’ eligibility is based solely on whether they are “high-income” according to

item.title: Continuous Improvement Through Evaluation: Building Better and Better Generative AI Interventions
item.description: How do you evaluate a development intervention based on generative AI, like a tutoring tool or a chatbot to provide advice to farmers? A technical lead might say that evaluating a generative AI tool means getting the model to perform well, putting safeguards in place, and shipping it. An economist m

item.title: Legal Status Alone Doesn’t Make Migrants Settle: Evidence from Venezuela
item.description: Hand a migrant a visa, the thinking goes, and the rest will follow: A formal job, tax payments, and eventually, a decision to stay. Many policies that aim to regularize undocumented immigrants are built on that premise. But new analysis , based on a survey of about 3,000 Venezuelan migrants across n

item.title: Making Schools Safe: Ministerial Taskforce Renews Commitment to End Violence in and Around Schools
item.description: Hon Conrad Sackey, Minister of Basic and Senior Secondary Education, Sierra Leone: “Every child should be free from violence. Safe and secure learning environments are core to well-being and growth, and we are committed to prioritizing the education sector’s role to ensure our children can thrive.”

item.title: Tax Reform in the Middle East After Conflict
item.description: The conflict in the Middle East will weigh heavily on public finances across the region for years to come . Destruction of public and private assets will ripple through economies in multiple ways: private consumption will likely weaken, while trade, tourism, and business confidence could suffer sign

item.title: The Atlas of Innovation: A New Tool for Navigating the Innovation Funding Landscape
item.description: The United States government spends close to $200 billion annually on research and development. Every dollar counts, as innovation is crucial to solving pressing social problems and the ultimate driver of economic growth. To make the most of R&D funding, policymakers need to consider not just what t

item.title: Seize the Moment: Raise Energy Taxes When Oil Prices Fall
item.description: The surge in international oil prices following the conflict in the Middle East has imposed an unwelcome supply shock on the world economy . While many advanced economies, particularly in Europe, used their fiscal space to cushion households and firms from higher fuel costs, this option was unavaila

item.title: How Much Aid is Actually Spent Abroad?
item.description: How much aid is actually spent abroad? That’s an important question, and we think we need a clear way to measure and talk about it. But Official Development Assistance (ODA) is no longer a good measure of overseas or international aid or development. To that end, we have coordinated with UK developm

item.title: A Book Review of the “Handbook on Child Poverty and Inequality”
item.description: Child poverty affects millions— recent estimates suggest more than 400 million children live in families earning less than $3 per day, and the number of children in poverty doubles if one considers other deprivations beyond income (for example, lack of access to education or sanitation). In the rece

item.title: Round 2: Up to $5 Million for Research on Global Lead Poisoning
item.description: Momentum on tackling global lead poisoning continues to grow, but there’s still much that we don’t know. So, we’re launching a new call for proposals, jointly with Coefficient Giving, to support research to help understand and mitigate the global burden of lead poisoning. We have up to $5 million to

item.title: A Standard Deviation of What? Rethinking How We Report Learning Gains
item.description: A donor comparing two reading programmes might reasonably start with the headline numbers. A programme in Nigeria reports an effect on learning outcomes of 0.42 standard deviations and is described as “highly effective and cost-effective.” A programme in Colombia reports a much smaller effect at com

item.title: CGD Podcast: Ending Violence in Schools with Dipak Naker and Gabriela Smarrelli
item.description: Globally, 115 million children are affected by school-related violence, from corporal punishment and bullying to sexual violence. Yet this issue has long been underfunded and underreported. Research shows that violence impedes children's learning , mental health, and long-term life outcomes. So how

item.title: A $24,000 Text Message Tweak Made a National School Program Work
item.description: A few text messages per school, costing under $1 per student, turned a national education program in Tanzania from one that failed to improve learning into one that succeeded. This finding shows how a small tweak in the implementation of a government program can make a big difference. The text messa

item.title: Giving Credit Where Credit Is Due: A Look at the World Bank’s New Guarantee Framework
item.description: World Bank Group President Ajay Banga has made the increased use of guarantees a key objective of his reform agenda, committing to a significant uptake in their use and facilitating this uptake by merging all instruments under the Multilateral Investment Guarantee Facility (MIGA), an arm of the Worl
